新产品功能不断推出，以扩大平台的吸引力。他们会工作吗？
   现在很容易忘记，但是曾经有一段时间，Twitter的流行观点是名人和粉丝们在很大程度上是轻描淡写的平台。是的，Twitter始终具有严肃的一面-它在2011年阿拉伯之春和2014年弗格森抗议活动中发挥了重要作用-以及它的阴暗面，包括猖anonymous的种族主义和来自匿名帐户的厌恶情绪。但在2016年之前，它被人们称为政治和社会文化战场，而不是愚蠢的模因和流行文化时刻的聚集地，例如2014年Ellen DeGeneres拍摄的奥斯卡自拍照打破了有史以来分享次数最多的推文记录。
 同样容易忘记的是，在2016年，Twitter的前景令人怀疑。它的用户群停滞不前；它正在赔钱；最糟糕的是，它无法弄清楚它想要成为什么样子。一个消极关注感兴趣主题的地方？直播活动的第二个屏幕？本身就是直播视频的中心吗？它的尝试似乎并不重要：正如Verge标题在2016年所说的那样，“ Twitter在做什么都没有用。”该公司的前景是如此黯淡，以至于正在征求公司收购的要约，但没人愿意买。迪士尼之所以通过，是因为它发现Twitter太“讨厌”。在首席执行官马克·贝尼奥夫（Marc Benioff）伤膝盖并将其视为一种天体信号之后，Salesforce屈服了。
 然后是唐纳德·特朗普的选举，四年，一切都转身。但是那四年快到了。 Twitter是否准备好在没有高音喇叭的情况下蓬勃发展？一系列即将到来的产品变更，包括本周推出的“舰队”，将测试其扩大其吸引力的能力，超越意识形态的Thunderdome或民主灭亡的新闻报导。
   特朗普的总统职位可能挽救了Twitter。 Twitter的阴谋和MAGA标签助长了候选人的竞选资格，他开始依赖该平台作为他与公众和支持者的直接联系。它不仅让他绕开了传统媒体，而且还绕开了自己的白宫通讯机构，其间带有煽动性，随意打断的推文，这些推文每天都在推动国家新闻周期乃至全球事务。反过来，当左派试图在自己的数字地盘上反击特朗普时，这产生了在线“抵抗”。如果推特是特朗普反动民粹主义的沃土，那么它对亚历山大·奥卡西奥·科尔特斯等进步民粹主义者同样有帮助。
 如果不是特朗普挽救了Twitter，则可能是算法时间表。很难夸大Twitter在2016年中期从逆时针供稿转换为Facebook新闻供稿样式算法的影响，该算法向每个用户显示其想看的内容。它不仅提高了参与度，而且还可能放大了两极分化和煽情主义的动态，并使该平台更容易被机器人，巨魔，宣传家和煽动者（包括特朗普）利用。确实，特朗普在Twitter的统治地位和算法时间表的成功似乎相互交织在一起。
 Twitter成为一场地震文化大战的地基，这也并非偶然，这迫使Overton窗口打开了曾经被左右左右激进的想法。即使新纳粹巨魔在右侧激增，＃MeToo和“黑色生活问题”抗议活动仍使Twitter成为民权运动复兴的先锋。不管是好是坏，Twitter从来没有像现在这样重要，尤其是在其本国的美国市场上。
它显示在公司的底线上，该底线在过去四年中一直稳定增长。在2016年中期的最低点，Twitter的估值不到100亿美元。如今，它的价值是其三倍以上。除了今年早些时候因冠状病毒引起的广告崩溃外，该公司自2017年以来就一直盈利。在2019年，该公司不再计算每月的休闲用户（这个数字一直在逐渐减少），并改为“可获​​利的每日活跃用户”。不断壮大的较小核心小组。这一举动表明，Twitter完全放弃了迪克·科索托（Dick Costolo）时代成为被动消费大众媒体的概念，并接受了杰克·多尔西（Jack Dorsey）关于建立一个互动平台的愿景，该互动平台建立在不仅潜伏但参与的惯常用户之上。
 至此，Twitter对公众人物，品牌和媒体的重要性已毋庸置疑。但是随着特朗普计划在一月份卸任（一个希望！），自2016年以来一直吸引着公众的政治马戏团中的主角角色已不再确定。拜登在许多方面都是反特朗普的，他的诺言的一部分是恢复常态，这将使美国和世界能够关闭Twitter和有线新闻并继续生活。他在社交媒体上并不在家，似乎极不可能发布让世界震惊的流氓推文。 Twitter将继续成为华盛顿的“水冷器”，但它将不再是超级大国政治活动的主要舞台。
 特朗普一旦辞职，他本人可能会也可能不会在Twitter上生存。正如《华盛顿邮报》和其他报道所报道的那样，他将失去公司在担任总统期间为全球领导人精心打造的Twitter规则的特殊豁免（而且，面对现实，很大程度上是因为他担任总统）。这意味着他可能会被禁止或停职，因为他任职四年来一直担任同样的职务。押注于特朗普以改变自己的位置似乎是一个很差的赌注，因此这可能归结于Twitter的挖苦意愿，并有可能让他和他的MAGA军团被竞争对手Parler击败。
 在这种背景下，Twitter的下一整套产品变更至少可以部分视为该公司为特朗普后的未来做准备。这并不是说它们背后的意图是，其中一些更新已经酝酿了很长时间。但这是他们到达的背景，这使他们的成功对于一个在时代精神中处于危险地位的社交网络至关重要。
 舰队是一个有趣的开始。从产品开发的角度来看，虽然几乎没有创新-它们基本上只是Twitter的Instagram Stories版本，而后者本身就是Snapchat Stories的副本-但是，他们改变了以支配方式使用Twitter的经验。狗的照片，自拍视频，流行文化参考和扔掉的模因在最初的日子里充满了“车队线”，与许多用户的家庭时间表的持续恐慌和愤怒形成了鲜明的对比。许多人散发出一种业余爱好或热诚，这可能在另一种情况下显得格格不入，但与Twitter的其余部分并置时令人耳目一新。
 最有希望的是，Fleets通过设计邀请用户以私密的方式相互发消息，而不是公开喜欢或转发彼此的内容。这是一种更加亲密，人性化的联系方式，从来都不是Twitter的强项-可能是因为您关注的很多人​​可能是陌生人而不是朋友。一旦新颖性逐渐消失，舰队和他们刺激的DM可能会消失，从而使该功能降级为具有小众吸引力的附加组件的状态，例如之前的Periscope和Vine。但是，如果它确实具有持久力，并且像Stories在Snapchat和Instagram上那样成为Twitter体验的核心，那么Fleets可以使Twitter体验更丰富，更多样化，既有趣又省力。
 Twitter在本周与记者的电话会议中嘲笑说，Spaces具有类似的潜力，即将推出。 Twitter似乎是Twitter对蓬勃发展的初创公司Clubhouse的回应，Spaces允许用户创建音频聊天室，进行仅邀请对象的对话，任何人都可以收听。值得注意的是，该功能将首先提供给少数群体的妇女和边缘化社区的人-感觉就像是Clubhouse的一个恰当的推文，其早期用户群偏向男性和特权人群。
与Fleets一样，Spaces的与众不同之处也不在于产品创意本身，而是它在Twitter的背景下可以发挥的作用，具有公共性和不对称的追随者网络。可以想象，太空会像奥斯卡的自拍照一样利用类似的文化脉络，也就是说，名人可以以一种自发，个性化的方式相互交流，这是普通粉丝熟悉和独特的。或许他们会变得更像是小组讨论的社交媒体版本，虽然会不太令人兴奋，但可能更符合Twitter过去四年的发展方向。
 Fleets and Spaces暗示的是可以想象到的不久的将来，比如说2022年的Twitter与2020年的Twitter根本不同。Twitter自己的目标是围绕“健康对话”的思想，但这似乎也许对于今天足以说明两极对立的平台来说，这是一座太遥远的桥梁。可以想象的是，Twitter变成了一种更具社交性的网络，在其中，乐趣和轻浮的角色与政治一起恢复了应有的地位。
 然而，即使这样，对于Twitter进行自我改造的能力以及现实世界中的事件，都可能过于乐观。地狱，我们现在甚至还不确定特朗普将离任。但是我们知道，如果他这样做的话，他所放的火仍将燃烧。当整个世界都处于黑暗的时间表上时，很难想象Twitter会以其他方式出现。一个更安全的选择可能是Twitter从根本上仍然是我们今天所知并讨厌的Twitter-不断发生的想法和部落冲突，一次又上瘾又模糊不清-也许特朗普少了一些，好吧，无论如何这是我们接下来要争取的。
   据BuzzFeed News报道，Facebook的事实核对标签并不能在减缓与选举相关的错误信息的传播方面起到很大作用。报告称，记者克雷格·西尔弗曼（Craig Silverman）和瑞安·麦克（Ryan Mac）从Facebook的数据科学家那里获得了信息，这些数据表明，将选举声明贴上有争议的标签，平均使股票的回购速度平均降低了8％左右。根据《纽约时报》的凯文·罗斯（Kevin Roose）的说法，这可以解释为什么上周在Facebook上最吸引人的20个帖子中包含“选举”一词的人全都来自特朗普，尽管每个人都贴有事实检查标签。 （我在上周的时事通讯中了解了罗斯的Facebook参与度数据。）这对Facebook而言并不是偶然的：据信息杂志的亚历克斯·希思（Alex Heath）称，该公司的公民诚信部门已建议采取措施减缓错误信息的传播，但首席执行官马克·扎克伯格（Mark Zuckerberg）拒绝了他们。同时，我的OneZero同事Sarah Emerson报告说，尽管有一些试图清除它们的“停止窃取”组织仍在Facebook上蓬勃发展。
 《华尔街日报》报道，面对反托拉斯调查，苹果将小型开发商的App Store收入削减比例从30％降低至15％，而这些开发商每年的清算费用不足100万美元。这是一种可喜的姿态，苹果公司迅速宣称这将帮助使用该平台的“绝大多数”开发人员。然而，正如《纽约时报》所指出的那样，那些小型开发商所占的收入不到苹果App Store收入的5％。换句话说，对于苹果来说，这是一种赚钱的好方法，并且可以缓解其面临的一些监管压力。这也很好地提醒了我们，即使是更强大的反托拉斯机制的威胁也足以开始改变公司的行为。 （我在二月份就针对苹果的反托拉斯论点作了深入的论述。）
          （推文中的屏幕截图显示了Facebook通知，鼓励用户阅读唐纳德·特朗普的帖子，事实也被Facebook标记为错误信息。）
  1968年，IBM解雇了从事计算机开发的先驱林恩·康威（Lynn Conway），因为他是变性人，在芯片开发方面取得了重要进展。 52年后的秋天，该公司邀请Conway参加一个正式道歉的活动，并授予她终身成就奖。
note_en:
New product features are rolling out to broaden the platform’s appeal. Will they work?
   It’s easy to forget now, but there was a time when a popular view of Twitter was that of a largely f rivolous platform for celebrities and their fans. Yes, Twitter has always had its serious side — it played notable roles in the  Arab Spring circa 2011 and the  Ferguson protests of 2014 — and its dark side, including  rampant racism and misogyny from anonymous accounts. But before 2016, it was known less as a political and sociocultural battleground than as a venue for silly memes and pop-culture moments, like the  Oscars selfie snapped by Ellen DeGeneres in 2014 that broke the record for the most-shared tweet of all time.
 Also easy to forget is that, in 2016, Twitter’s future was in doubt. Its user base had stagnated; it was losing money; and worst of all, it couldn’t figure out what it wanted to be. A place to passively follow  topics of interest? A  second screen for live events? A hub for live video in its own right? It didn’t seem to matter what it tried: As a  Verge headline put it in 2016, “ nothing Twitter is doing is working.” The company’s outlook was so bleak that it was soliciting offers for a corporate buyout — except nobody wanted to buy it. Disney passed because  it found Twitter “too nasty”; Salesforce bowed out after CEO Marc Benioff hurt his knee and took it as  a sort of celestial sign.
 Then came the election of Donald Trump, and for four years, everything turned around. But that four years is almost up. Is Twitter ready to thrive without the tweeter-in-chief? A series of coming product changes, including  this week’s launch of Fleets, will test its ability to broaden its appeal beyond that of an ideological Thunderdome or a news ticker for the demise of democracy.
   Trump’s presidency may have saved Twitter. A president whose candidacy had been fueled by Twitter conspiracies and the MAGA hashtag came to rely on the platform as his direct line to the public and his supporters. It let him bypass not just traditional media outlets but his own White House communications apparatus with incendiary, haphazardly punctuated tweets that drove the national news cycle on a daily basis — and even, at times, global affairs. That, in turn, spawned an online “resistance” as the left sought to counter Trump on his own digital turf. If Twitter was fertile ground for Trump’s reactionary populism, it has been equally instrumental for progressive populists such as Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez.
 If it wasn’t Trump that saved Twitter, it may have been the algorithmic timeline. It’s hard to overstate the effects of  Twitter’s mid-2016 shift from a reverse-chronological feed to a Facebook News Feed-style algorithm that shows each user what it thinks they’ll want to see. It not only boosted engagement but likely amplified the dynamics of polarization and sensationalism and made the platform more exploitable by bots, trolls, propagandists, and demagogues — including Trump. Indeed, it seems likely that  Trump’s dominance of Twitter and the success of the algorithmic timeline were intertwined, with each reinforcing the other.
 It’s also no coincidence that Twitter has become ground zero for a seismic culture war that has forced open the Overton window to ideas once considered radical on both the right and left. Even as neo-Nazi trolls proliferated on the right, #MeToo and the Black Lives Matter protests put Twitter in the vanguard of a resurgent civil rights movement. For better or worse,  Twitter has never been more relevant, especially in its home market of the United States, which remains its largest and by far its most lucrative.
 It  shows in the company’s bottom line, which has trended steadily upward for exactly four years. At its mid-2016 nadir, Twitter was valued at less than $10 billion. Today, it’s worth more than three times that. With the exception of a coronavirus-induced ad crash earlier this year, the company  has been profitable since 2017. In 2019, it stopped counting casual monthly users — a number that had been slowly shrinking — and switched to “ monetizable daily active users,” a narrower core group that has  steadily expanded. That move signaled that Twitter had fully abandoned the Dick Costolo-era notion of becoming a mass medium for passive consumption and embraced Jack Dorsey’s vision of an interactive platform built on habitual users who don’t just lurk but participate.
 By this point, Twitter’s importance to public figures, brands, and the media is not in question. But with Trump slated to leave office (one hopes!) in January, its starring role in the political circus that has captivated the public since 2016 is no longer assured. Biden is in many ways the anti-Trump, and part of his promise is to restore a normalcy that will allow America and the world to turn off Twitter and cable news and get on with their lives. He is not at home on social media and seems highly unlikely to post rogue tweets that set the world abuzz.  Twitter will remain Washington’s watercooler, but it will no longer be the main stage on which a superpower’s politics play out.
 Trump himself may or may not survive on Twitter once he’s out of office. As the  Washington Post and others have reported,  he’ll lose the special exemptions from Twitter’s rules that the company carved out for world leaders during his presidency (and, let’s face it, largely because  of his presidency). That means he could be banned or suspended from the service for the same kinds of posts that he’s been churning out for four years. Betting on Trump to change his spots would seem a poor wager, so it will probably come down to Twitter’s willingness to dig in and risk losing him and his MAGA army to a rival, such as  Parler.
 Against that backdrop,  Twitter’s next suite of product changes can be seen at least partly as the company’s preparation for a post-Trump future. This is not to say that’s the intent behind them, per se — some of these updates have been brewing for a long time. But it is the context in which they arrive, and it makes their success feel pivotal for a social network whose place in the zeitgeist may be at risk.
 Fleets are an interesting start. While hardly innovative from a product development standpoint — they’re basically just Twitter’s version of Instagram Stories, which was itself a clone of Snapchat Stories — they shook up the experience of using Twitter in a bracing way. The dog pictures, selfie videos, pop-culture references, and tossed-off memes that filled the “fleet line” in its first days formed a stark contrast to the ceaseless panic and outrage of many users’ home timelines. Many exuded an amateurishness or earnestness that might feel grating in another context but refreshing in juxtaposition to the rest of Twitter.
 Most promisingly, Fleets by design invite users to privately message one another with personal replies as opposed to publicly liking or retweeting each other’s content.  It’s a more intimate, humane form of connection that has never been Twitter’s strong suit — probably because a lot of the people you follow are likely to be strangers rather than friends. It’s possible that Fleets and the DMs they spur will die down once the novelty wears off, relegating the feature to the status of an add-on with niche appeal, like Periscope and Vine before it. But if it does have staying power and becomes core to the Twitter experience in the way that Stories have on Snapchat and Instagram, Fleets could make for a fuller, more varied Twitter experience that is both more fun and less exhausting.
 There’s a similar potential for Spaces, a “coming-soon” feature that Twitter teased in a call with journalists this week.  In what appears to be Twitter’s answer to the buzzy startup Clubhouse, Spaces will allow users to create audio chat rooms, holding invite-only conversations that anyone can listen in on. Notably, the feature will be offered first to a small group of women and people from marginalized communities — which feels like a well-placed subtweet of Clubhouse, whose early user base skews male and privileged.
 As with Fleets, what’s distinctive about Spaces is not the product idea itself but the role it could play in the context of Twitter, with its public nature and asymmetric follower networks.  One could imagine Spaces tapping a similar cultural vein as the Oscars selfie — that is, a venue for celebrities to interact with each other in a spontaneous, personal way that is both familiar to and uniquely accessible to the average fan. Or perhaps they’ll turn out to be more like the social-media version of a panel discussion, which would be less exciting but perhaps more in keeping with the direction Twitter has taken over the past four years.
 What Fleets and Spaces hint at is a conceivable near-future in which the Twitter of, say, 2022 feels fundamentally different from the Twitter of 2020. Twitter’s own articulation of its goal revolves around the idea of “healthy conversations,” but that seems like maybe a bridge too far for a platform that today exemplifies pretty much the polar opposite. What is possible to imagine is that  Twitter becomes a somewhat more social network, one in which the roles of fun and frivolity are restored to their rightful place alongside politics.
 Yet even that might be too optimistic, both about Twitter’s ability to reinvent itself and about the course of real-world events. Hell, we don’t even know for sure at this point that Trump will  leave office. But we know that if and when he does, the fires he set will still be burning.  When the whole world feels like it’s on a dark timeline, it’s hard to imagine Twitter’s being any other way. A safer bet might be that Twitter remains fundamentally the Twitter that we know and love-hate today — a constant skirmish of ideas and tribes, at once addictive and vaguely monotonous — with perhaps a bit less Trump and a bit more of, well, whatever it is we’ll be fighting about next.
   Facebook’s fact-checking labels aren’t doing much to slow the spread of election-related misinformation,   BuzzFeed News reported. Reporters Craig Silverman and Ryan Mac obtained information from Facebook’s data scientists suggests that labeling election claims as disputed slowed re-shares by only about 8% on average, according to the report. That helps explain why, per the  New York Times’ Kevin Roose, the past week’s 20 most-engaged Facebook posts containing the word “election”  were all from Trump, even though every single one carried a fact-checking label. (I dove into Roose’s Facebook engagement data in  last week’s newsletter.) This was not an accident on Facebook’s part: According to  The Information’s Alex Heath, the company’s civic integrity unit had recommended measures to slow the spread of misinformation, but  CEO Mark Zuckerberg rejected them. Meanwhile,  “Stop the Steal” groups are still flourishing on Facebook despite some attempts to purge them, my  OneZero colleague Sarah Emerson reported.
 Facing antitrust probes,  Apple lowered its cut of App Store revenues from 30% to 15% for small developers clearing less than $1 million per year,  the  Wall Street Journal reported. It’s a welcome gesture, and Apple was quick to tout that it would help the “vast majority” of developers who use its platform. And yet, as the  New York Times noted, those small developers account for less than 5% of Apple’s App Store revenues. In other words, it’s a cheap way for Apple to earn some good will and perhaps defuse some of the regulatory pressure it’s facing. It’s also a good reminder that even the threat of a more robust antitrust regime is enough to start changing companies’ behavior. (I wrote in depth about the  antitrust argument against Apple in February.)
          (Screenshots in the tweet show a Facebook notification encouraging a user to read a post from Donald Trump, which turned out to also be labeled by Facebook as misinformation.)
  In 1968, IBM fired  Lynn Conway, a pioneering computer scientist who made key advances in chip development, for being transgender. This fall, 52 years later, the company invited Conway to an event at which it  formally apologized and gave her a lifetime achievement award.
title: Twitter在特朗普担任总统期间发挥了核心作用，它正试图通过推出“舰队”（Fleets）之类的功能，重新定义特朗普后的未来
title_en: Twitter, which played a central role in Trump's presidency, is attempting to redefine itself for a post-Trump future through the launch of features like Fleets

note:
上图是Offenbacher Schrift的示例，这是由字体设计师RudolfKoch（以及令人遗憾的是热情的德国民族主义者）设计的手写风格。 Offenbacher Schriftwas可以说是古老的德国手写风格的最后化身，被称为Kurrent，数百年来，德语区一直受到这种辐射，直到纳粹以某种历史上有趣的方式将其改写。它也与苏特林（Sütterlin）密切相关，后者是科伦特（Kurrent）最终开花的主要形式。
 库伦特语的吸引人和有趣的特质之一，尤其是与英语草书相比，是他们对交叉和重叠线条的厌恶。例如，小写字母c：在英语草书中，笔从左下角开始倾斜到c的顶部，然后沿同一条线向后翻倍以形成底部。在Sütterlinschrift中巧妙地避免了这一点，它选择了单个最小值。
 正是这种品质导致了Offenbacher最奢侈的特性。有很多吸引人的地方（我是一个人，他们在信的那些必须相互交叉的部分上也有很多困难;当写草书时，通常拿起笔并在我必须写时重新开始a，c，d，g，换句话说，当我必须从左下角到右上角以开始写字母时），但未经修改，Kurrent有点偏心英语。
     我们可以看到这里发生了什么。在每种情况下，字母都有一点缝隙：在第一行中，它向下弯曲，而在下一行中，它向上弯曲，但是在每种情况下，手都需要在笔划和笔直之间建立一定的距离。弯曲以确保它们彼此不邻接。
  对于我的需求，这是反对将线混合到不健康的极端的狂热。我要避免为了开始而不得不越过aletter的身体；另一方面，我的y摩擦的圆头和直头彼此非常舒服。毕竟是2020年。
 对我们来说幸运的是，脚本的常规构造意味着我们可以应用同样常规的简化。在上述每种情况下，我们都可以简单地消除这种差距，并且这种规律性保证了我们最终不会出现脚本混乱或不一致的情况。
  这是我的一个非常好的折衷方案，它使我不喜欢Kurrent保留了很多我喜欢的常规性和整洁性，同时又使字母通常更简单，更易于识别。
可以说，上面的一个异常是r的处理。不一定我不认为该原理适用-我认为底部的短弓形冲程是为了避免下冲程和上冲程混合而插入的间隙，并且我认为可以与其他方法完全消除是-只是r字符是如此之小，以至于偶然地或非偶然地，该字母中的间隙笔画都比其他字符中突出。尤其是当您考虑到最后一个笔划（向上笔划后的弯曲笔直）将是下一个字母的连接符（如果r不是最后一个）时，人们可能会提出这样的论据，即从视觉上讲，r的间隙笔触变成了字母的主要图形特征并消除它是从根本上改变其字符。
 本着这种可能的反对精神，我想提供孤立的r形式的替代词I，我从它的爱尔兰起源中摘出了该词，发现它是一个很好的匹配：
  它之所以能很好地工作，是因为其结构几乎与Kurrent e相同，它具有两个直立冲程，并且其连接冲程在第一个冲程的中间或更大处开始[1]。对于岛r，我们只需给第一个笔划降序。两者共享一个角色的程度-尽管很明显可以区分-告诉我，替换是很自然的替换。
 当然，重要的是连接器从中间开始而不是从底部开始，因为否则它的形状就像n。 ↩
note_en:
Above is a sample of  Offenbacher Schrift, a style of handwritingdesigned by the type designer (and, regrettably, enthusiastic Germannationalist),  RudolfKoch. Offenbacher Schriftwas arguably the final incarnation of the venerable German handwritingstyle known as  Kurrent, atradition particular to German-speaking areas for hundreds of years,up until the Nazis fucked it up in some historically interestingways. It’s closely related also to Sütterlin, which wasthe dominant form of Kurrent in its final flowering.
 One of the attractive and interesting qualities of the Kurrentscripts, especially in comparison to English cursive, is theirabhorrence of re-crossing and overlapping lines. For instance, thelower-case  c:  in English cursive, the pen, startingfrom the lower-left, slopes up to the top part of the c, and thendoubles back along the same line to make the bottom part. This isstudiously avoided in the Sütterlinschrift, which opts for the single minim.
 It’s also this quality that is responsible for Offenbacher’s mostextravagant peculiarities. There’s a lot that’s attractive about it(I’m someone who also has a lot of difficulty with those parts of theletter that have to cross over each other; when writing in cursive, Iusually pick up the pen and start again when I have to write an  a, c,  d,  g—in other words, when I have to cross from the bottomleft to the upper right in order to start a letter), but unmodified,Kurrent is a little too eccentric to the Anglophone eye.
     We can see what’s happening here. In each case, the letter hasacquired a little gap in it: in the top row, it bows downwards, and inthe bottom row, it bows upwards, but in each case, the hand needs toestablish a bit of distance between a straight stroke and a curve inorder to ensure they don’t abut each other.
  For my needs, this is the mania against the intermingling of linestaken to an unhealthy extreme. I want to avoid having to pass over aletter’s body in order to start it; on other hand, I’m perfectlycomfortable with the round bit and the straight bit of my  y rubbingup against each other. It’s the year 2020, after all.
 Luckily for us, the regular construction of the script means we canapply an equally regular simplification. We can simply eliminate thegap in each of the above cases, and the regularity of it guaranteesthat we won’t end up with a mangled or inconsistent script.
  This strikes me as a very fine compromise, having kept a lot of theregularity and cleanliness that I like out of Kurrent while making theletters generally a little simpler and more recognizable.
     Arguably, the one outlier in the above is the treatment of  r. It’snot necessarily that I don’t think the principle applies—I thinkthat the short, bowed stroke on the bottom is a gap inserted to avoidthe downstroke and the upstroke intermingling, and I think it can beeliminated in exactly the same way as the others are—it’s just thatthe  r character is so minimal that, by accident or not, thegap-stroke is much more prominent in that letter than in theothers. Especially when you consider that the last stroke, the curvingone after the upstroke, will be the connector to the next letter (ifthe  r isn’t last), one could make the argument that, visuallyspeaking, the gap-stroke of the  r becomes the dominant graphicalfeature of the letter and to eliminate it is to fundamentally alterits character.
 In the spirit of that possible objection, I want to offer analternative in the form of the   insular r, ꞃ, which I haveplucked out of its Irish origin and found to be a good match:
  It works very well because its construction is almost identical tothat of the Kurrent  e, with its two upright strokes and itsconnecting stroke beginning midway-or-greater up the first one [1]. Inthe case of the insular r, we simply give the first stroke adescender. The degree to which the two share a character—while beingvery clearly distinguishable—tells me that the substitution is quitea natural one.
 Of course, it’s important that the connector starts midway up,and not at the bottom, because otherwise it would be shaped justlike an  n.   ↩
title: Offenbacher字体的常规简化
title_en: A Regular Simplification of Offenbacher Schrift

note:
为了弥补第二次世界大战期间日本的人手不足，该国的军事指挥官经常将囚犯运到日本大陆，在那里他们在采矿和重工业中担任奴隶劳工。
 作为1941年被我俘虏后进行过这样的旅行的人，我可以证明，前往日本的旅程本身就是恐怖的。由于日本人没有在囚犯运输船上标上红叉或其他商定的标志，因此，当毫无戒心的美国潜艇错误地用鱼雷袭击了其中一些运输工具时，成千上万的囚犯在海上丧生。 1942年10月1日，一艘名为“里斯本丸”（Lisbon Maru）的船只从香港飞往日本，载有约1,800艘英国战俘，是由美国潜艇USS石斑鱼沉没在南中国海的，他的船长不知道那艘珍贵的货物。保持。八百四十六名囚犯死于溺水，或被日本人射杀，当时他们试图游过残骸。
  1943年1月19日，第一批1,180名同盟囚犯（其中包括我本人为663名加拿大人）乘坐未加标记的日本运输船Tatsuta Maru离开香港。囚犯被紧紧地塞进了货舱的一部分，以致我们只能轮班躺下。卫生间设施包括从上面放下的大水桶。为期四天的旅行因痢疾流行而特别地令人难堪。当我们到达日本时，我们被脱水，半饿并被污物覆盖。有人告诉我们，我们的目的地是（当时新成立的）东京3D鹤见战俘营，该营地设在日本横滨的Nippon Kokan工业设施，当时是该国最大的造船厂。 （在整个战争过程中，该营地使用了几个不同的名称。）离开船后，我们乘火车被带到距营地约一英里的营地。一两天后，我们开始工作-安排13天，然后休息1天。
 日本Kokan院子很大。它为帝国海军生产民用舰艇和海军舰艇，而当时日本的舰只经常被美国潜艇击沉。尽管美国轰炸机无数次袭击，但这个至关重要的设施遭受的损失却很小，这令我们所有人感到惊讶。还有我的加拿大战俘之一，参谋长查尔斯·阿尔弗雷德（“查理”）克拉克（Charles Alfred（“查理”）克拉克），他在成为日本囚犯之前曾在香港“ C”部队总部总部工作）开始意识到卑微的囚徒可能会成功轰炸机舰队没有的地方
 克拉克是一个不寻常的人，是在两次世界大战中都服役的少数士兵之一。克拉克（Clark）生于英国，于1915年移居加拿大，随后他应征入伍，在维米里奇（Vimy Ridge）受了伤。在加拿大邮政服务了二十多年的平民生活后，他于1940年再次入伍，现年44岁，并被调派到香港。在那里，他服役于1941年12月8日遭到日军袭击的小型盟军驻军，距珍珠港袭击事件仅四个小时。根据他的杰出行为引用：
 参谋长加拿大邮政总队的克拉克（Clark）在香港“ C”部队总部。他住所所在的建筑物受到重型炮弹的直接袭击。他的一名军官被打死，“ C”部队第二司令部DSO，MC的Patrick Hennessy上校受到致命伤害。在另一个NCO的协助下，（比尔·欧弗顿）Staff Sgt。克拉克在轩尼诗上校的腿上套上止血带，将他放在门上，然后将他抬到铁梯下的一个地方以确保安全。参谋长克拉克寻求帮助，不得不经过一栋燃烧的建筑物，里面装有300,000发小武器弹药。飞行子弹和敌人炮弹的危险并没有阻止Staff Sgt。克拉克（Clark）爬过这种拦河坝，到达了奥斯丁山（Mount Austin）军营，那里派了一辆救护车。在同样危险的情况下，他与一名医务人员返回轩尼诗上校。然后是参谋长。克拉克协助担当担架的轩尼诗上校随身带到救护车并在大火中送往医院。
 作为囚犯，克拉克知道，对造船厂的一次成功破坏活动（实际上是一项“内部工作”）将极大地帮助盟军的战争努力。但是他也知道，对于任何被捕甚至对日本Kokan实施最小规模敌对行动的人，后果都是严重的。因此，保密是至关重要的。但是，当他开始策划破坏活动时，克拉克意识到自己至少需要一个值得信赖的帮凶。经过深思熟虑，他与加拿大皇家军械公司的斯坦利·卡梅隆私人联系，这是一个明智的选择。的确，直到行动完成之后，我们其余的人才知道他们的伙伴关系。
造船厂是一个庞大且高度复杂的工业系统。 1943年，在计算机自​​动化时代之前，基本的生产过程可大致概括如下。一名海洋工程师将以蓝图的形式设计船舶部件的图纸。然后将这些零件渲染到木制模具或模板中，以用来在厚钢板上描绘出每个零件的形状，从而引导用于将钢切割成所需形式的乙炔炬。从钢板切割出每个零件后，将其转移到正在建造的适当容器中，并进行铆接，焊接或螺栓固定。造船厂生产的船舶数量巨大，每个船中包含大量零件，这意味着必须在现场存储大量纸质蓝图和模板。储存它们的两座建筑的设施在一个工业造船过程中包括一个微小的神经中枢，否则会散布到许多平方英里的地方，这对任何想突然停止生产的人来说都是相当大的兴趣。
 关键的问题是如何。由于院子每天工作12小时，因此Clark制定的任何计划都必须在日光之下，在日军的保护下进行，这是不可能完成的任务。他需要的是某种方式来延迟破坏性行动的发展，以便在空旷的地方展开。
  在分配工作的地点，克拉克和卡梅隆开始将各种可燃物用于生产原始的炸弹，例如涂料稀释剂，油，碎布，浸油的刨花，汽油，树脂，纸和赛璐oid。为了正确计时，他们使用了一支蜡烛，该蜡烛一旦在下午点燃，便会燃烧数小时，直到点燃一个手工熔断器，然后引燃组装好的可燃物。
 克拉克和卡梅隆花了一年的时间-在没有其他人，甚至是战俘的不知情的情况下行动-进入目标建筑物，为炸弹建立了安全的藏身之地，走私可燃物，并将设备组装在一起，所有同时知道意外搜索或单个错误将导致某些甚至可能是令人毛骨悚然的死亡。
 勇敢地采取行动来挽救自己或战友，这是一回事，虽然值得称赞，但通常是反思性本能的产物。克拉克和卡梅隆所做的一切需要一种持续的勇气。这两名杰出的加拿大士兵冒着一切风险将战斗带到敌人心脏地带的中心。
 到1944年1月中旬，克拉克和卡梅伦已经准备好平息香港发生的一切。在1月20日下午辞职之前不久，他们将设备放在一堆垃圾后面，放在一间模具房中鲜为人知的储藏室中，点燃了临时的蜡烛保险丝，然后与同志们一起回到3D营地。当天晚上8时，按照计划，每个战俘都在营地中并进行了核算，蜡烛燃烧到了保险丝，炸弹被点燃。仓库和附近的蓝图设施被火焰吞没了。由于可燃物的性质，最初的白炽烈火散发出大量的热量和烟气，现场的少数安全人员无力扑灭很快变成熊熊的地狱。
 结果就是克拉克和卡梅伦希望的一切：在庞大的造船厂停工。在未来的日子里，对已经接近完工的船只进行了一些有限的建造，但是建造新船已成为不可能。其中包括日本海军迫切需要的反潜舰。
说日本人很生气是轻描淡写。令人恐惧的日本军事警察肯平泰（Kenpeitai）很快就来到我们的营地，对加拿大军官和他们怀疑可能涉及的任何人提出质疑。但是有两件事阻止了他们发现真相。首先，克拉克和卡梅伦的秘密条约已经达成共识。没有人（包括我）告诉警察任何东西，因为除了这对阴谋者之外，没有人可以告诉。 （事实上​​，直到战争结束，我才发现事实。）其次，大火消耗了证据。克拉克和卡梅伦没有留下任何可以追溯到他们或任何其他战俘工作人员的明显信号。
 我们也很清楚地知道，尽管引起了轩然大波，但船厂负责人和营地指挥官并没有您想像的那样焦虑，因为发现他们的指挥下的战俘犯有纵火罪-因为这样的发现几乎肯定会导致他们自己的军事法庭，甚至可能死亡。 （此外，我们的无罪主张与日本人的观点相吻合，即我们战俘既没有技巧也没有勇气进行这种大胆的绝技。）因此，更安全的做法是至少在正式意义上得出结论，认为罪魁祸首是自发的燃烧。由于没有犯规行为的证据，日本调查人员除了怀疑之外没有什么可做的了，重点转移到了重建设施的生产能力上。
 由于现在休眠的造船厂无需进行任何人工工作，囚犯被分割并转移到其他营地工作。 （就我而言，我最终来到了日本北部山区的Ohashi监狱营地，我在上一篇Quillette文章中描述了1945年的解放）。带着一两个残酷的笑声，我们离开了残破的造船厂及其困惑的日本指挥官。
 美国海军少尉爱德华五世·达克韦勒（Edward V. Dockweiler）在被释放后，在我们出发时在3D营地的盟军高级官员撰写了一份报告，报道了日寇向美国和加拿大军事当局发射大火。它部分读取为：
 1944年1月18日大约20:00，大火在该院子里发生，彻底摧毁了钢棚，船舶outfit装店，战俘食堂，索具大厅，工具室，部分装船工人的车间和模具车间。这时，院子里从事护航驱逐舰和商船的建造。其月产量约为8,000吨。大火由加拿大邮政总队的克拉克中士发起。和私人K.S.卡梅伦，加拿大皇家军械总队...这种破坏行为极大地破坏了该院子的生产，并直接最小化了日本的战争努力，而这两个人在战俘的阻碍下对盟军战争努力的贡献是不可能的。高估了。在我的管辖下，他们作为战俘的行为堪称典范，并符合加拿大军队的最高传统。
 克拉克中士和卡梅隆中士逃脱了侦查直到被囚禁结束，他们都幸存下来回到了加拿大家庭。为了勇敢的行动，他们被国王装饰。 “查理”克拉克（Charlie Clark）获得了杰出行为奖章（DCM）和卡梅伦私人军事奖章（MM）。
 克拉克（Clark）也成为返回加拿大士兵社区的充满活力的领袖，并最终成立了加拿大香港退伍军人协会。可悲的是，这个勇敢的人回到家后不久，在一场房屋大火中丧生。并与他一起灭亡，详细记录了他和卡梅伦如何勇敢地将战争推向了他们的敌人。
回到亚伯达省后，卡梅隆私人俱乐部以“大哥大”，“基瓦尼斯俱乐部”成员和加拿大国家盲人协会（CNIB）志愿者的身份活跃于他的社区，他以这种能力教会盲人打高尔夫球和卷曲运动。在撰写此报告时，我询问了他的家人关于他的战时功绩的情况。他们告诉我，只有在他被装饰并在新闻界写下来时，他们才知道这些。当被问及为什么他以前没有告诉他们这件事时，卡梅伦只是笑了笑，说他“只是在履行我的职责”（一种令人敬佩的谦虚措辞，正如他在1944年时代的日本俘虏所指出的那样，从字面上讲并不是真的） ）。
 今天是英联邦的纪念日，向退伍军人及其牺牲致敬。像在军队中服役的其他人一样，我目睹了许多勇敢无私的举动。但是，很少有人能像这两名被俘加拿大士兵那样令人钦佩和成功。
   乔治·麦克唐纳（George MacDonell）于1922年出生于艾伯塔省的埃德蒙顿。他曾在加拿大的皇家步枪队服役，该部队于1941年作为C-Force的一部分部署到香港，不久后被日本军队占领。关于他的故事的更多信息可以在这里和这里找到。
 特色图片：大和号战舰，拍摄于1941年9月，日本加入第二次世界大战之前。大和号是在广岛附近的吴市海军兵工厂建造的，而不是本文讨论的横滨-三菱造船厂的设施（历史照片几乎没有幸存）。
note_en:
To compensate for Japan’s manpower shortage during World War II, the country’s military commanders often shipped their prisoners to the Japanese mainland, where they worked as slave labourers in mining and heavy industry.
 As someone who made such a trip after my own capture in 1941, I can  attest that the journey to Japan was terrifying in and of itself. Because the Japanese didn’t mark  prisoner transport ships with a red cross or some other agreed-upon symbol, thousands of prisoners were lost at sea when unsuspecting American submarines mistakenly torpedoed some of these transports. One such ship, the  Lisbon Maru, carrying about 1,800 British POWs from Hong Kong to Japan, was sunk in the South China Sea on October 1st, 1942, by the American submarine USS Grouper, whose captain had no idea of the precious cargo in the hold. Eight hundred and forty-six prisoners died, either by drowning, or were shot by the Japanese as they tried to swim clear of the wreck.
  This was the backdrop when the first group of 1,180 Allied prisoners—including 663 Canadians, of which I was one—left Hong Kong on January 19th, 1943 on the unmarked Japanese transport steamer Tatsuta Maru. Prisoners were jammed into a section of the hold so tightly that we could lie down only in shifts. Toilet facilities consisted of large buckets lowered from above. The four-day trip was made particularly hellish by a dysentery epidemic that broke out. And when we arrived in Japan, we were dehydrated, half-starved, and covered with filth. Our destination, we were told, was the (then newly established)  Tokyo 3D Tsurumi Prisoner of War Camp, at the Nippon Kokan industrial facility in Yokohama, then the country’s biggest shipbuilding works. (The camp went by  several different names over the course of the war.) After leaving the ship, we were taken by train to our camp, which was about a mile from the shipyards. A day or two later, we began work—a schedule of 13 days on followed by one day off.
 The Nippon Kokan yard was huge. It produced both civilian ships and naval vessels for the Imperial Navy, this at a time when Japan’s ships were being regularly sunk by American submarines. It surprised us all how little damage this vital facility had suffered, despite numerous attacks by American bombers. And one of my fellow Canadian POWs—Staff Sergeant Charles Alfred (“Charlie”) Clark, who’d served in the Headquarters unit of “C” Force at Hong Kong before becoming a Japanese prisoner—began getting the idea that humble prisoners might succeed where fleets of bombers had not.
 Clark was an unusual individual, one of the few soldiers who served in both World Wars. Born in England, Clark emigrated to Canada in 1915, whereupon he enlisted in the army and was wounded at Vimy Ridge. Following two decades of civilian life with Canada’s postal service, he enlisted again in 1940, now 44 years old, and was deployed to Hong Kong. There, he served with the small Allied garrison that was attacked by the Japanese on December 8th, 1941, just four hours after the attack on Pearl Harbor. According to his Distinguished Conduct  citation:
 Staff Sgt. Clark of the Canadian Postal Corps was at Headquarters of “C” Force at Hong Kong. A building in which he was quartered received a direct hit from a heavy shell. One of his officers was killed and Col. Patrick Hennessy, DSO, MC, second in command of “C” Force, was mortally wounded. With the assistance of another NCO, (Bill Overton) Staff Sgt. Clark applied a tourniquet to Col. Hennessy’s legs, placed him on a door and carried him to a spot under an iron staircase for safety. Staff Sgt. Clark went for help and had to pass a blazing building which contained 300,000 rounds of small arms ammunition which was exploding. The danger of flying bullets and enemy shells did not deter Staff Sgt. Clark, who crept through this barrage and reached the Mount Austin barracks, where an ambulance was sent for. He returned to Col. Hennessy under the same dangerous conditions with a medical officer who treated him. Then Staff Sgt. Clark assisted in carrying Col. Hennessy on a stretcher to the ambulance and to hospital while under fire.
 As a prisoner, Clark knew that a successful sabotage attack on the shipyard—an “inside job,” as it were—would help the Allied war effort immensely. But he also knew that the consequences for anyone caught committing even the smallest hostile act against Nippon Kokan would be severe. The need for secrecy was therefore paramount. But as he began to plot his sabotage, Clark realized he’d need at least one trusted accomplice. After careful consideration, he approached Private Stanley Cameron of the Royal Canadian Ordnance Corps, which proved to be a prudent choice. Indeed, none of the rest of us knew about their partnership until well after the deed had been carried out.
 A shipyard is a massive and highly complex industrial system. In 1943, before the age of computer automation, the basic production process could be roughly summarized as follows. A marine engineer would design, in blueprint form, drawings of a ship’s components. These parts would then be rendered into wooden moulds or templates that could be used to trace out the shape of each part on thick steel plate, which would guide the acetylene torches used to cut the steel into required form. As each part was cut from steel sheets, it would be transferred to the appropriate vessel under construction and riveted, welded, or bolted into place. The high volume of ships being produced at the shipyard, and the large number of parts contained in each one, meant that tons of paper blueprints and templates had to be stored on site. The two-building facility where they were stored comprised a single tiny nerve center within an industrial shipbuilding process that otherwise spread out over many square miles—a fact of considerable interest to anyone who might want to bring production to a sudden halt.
 The crucial question was how. Since the yard worked on a 12-hour day shift, any scheme Clark developed would have to be executed in broad daylight, under the noses of Japanese guards—an impossible task. What he needed was some way to set the destructive act in motion on a delayed basis, so that it would unfold when the site was empty.
  At the work sites to which they’d been assigned, Clark and Cameron began squirreling away a variety of combustibles that they could use to produce a primitive firebomb—such as paint thinner, oil, rags, oil-soaked shavings, benzine, resin, paper, and celluloid. To time the operation properly, they used a candle, which, once lit in the afternoon, would burn down for several hours until it ignited a hand-made fuse that would, in turn, set off the assembled combustibles.
 It took Clark and Cameron a year—acting without the knowledge of anyone else, even fellow POWs—to gain access to the target buildings, establish a safe hiding place for the bomb, smuggle in the combustibles, and put the device together, all the while knowing that an unexpected search or a single mistake would lead to certain, and possibly gruesome, death.
 It is one thing to act courageously to save yourself or one’s comrades in the heat of the battle—which, while laudable, is typically a product of reflexive instinct. What Clark and Cameron did required a more sustained kind of courage. These two remarkable Canadian soldiers risked everything to take the fight to the centre of the enemy’s heartland.
 By mid-January, 1944, Clark and Cameron were ready to get even for what happened at Hong Kong. Shortly before quitting time on the afternoon of January 20th, they set up their device behind a pile of rubbish in one of the rarely inspected storerooms in the mould loft, lit the makeshift candle fuse, then marched back to Camp 3D with their comrades. At 8pm that night, with every POW in camp and accounted for, the candle burned down to the fuse and the bomb ignited, just as planned. The storage building and the neighbouring blueprint facility were engulfed in flame. Because of the nature of the combustibles, the initial incandescent blaze emitted an enormous amount of heat and smoke, and the handful of security officers on site were powerless to extinguish what soon became a raging inferno.
 The result was everything Clark and Cameron had hoped for: Work at the massive shipyard ground to a halt. In the days to come, some limited construction did recommence on ships that already were near completion, but building new ships had become impossible. This included the anti-submarine vessels that the Japanese navy desperately needed.
 To say that the Japanese were furious is an understatement. And the feared  Kenpeitai—Japan’s military police—were soon in our camp, questioning the Canadian warrant officers and anyone else they suspected might be involved. But two things blocked their discovery of the truth. First, the pact of secrecy Clark and Cameron had agreed to had been total. No one (including me) told the police anything because, aside from this pair of conspirators, no one had anything to tell. (In fact, I didn’t discover the facts until the war was over.) Secondly, the fire had consumed the evidence. Clark and Cameron left no telltale signs that could be traced to them or any of the other POW workers.
 It also became clear to us that, despite the uproar, the shipyard manager and camp commandant weren’t as anxious as you might think to find out that POWs under their command were guilty of arson—since such a finding almost certainly would have led to their own court martial and, possibly, death. (Moreover, our claims of innocence dovetailed with the Japanese view that we POWs would have neither the skill nor the courage to pull off such an audacious stunt.) And so the safer course was to conclude, at least officially, that the culprit was spontaneous combustion. With no evidence of foul play, Japanese investigators had little to go on except their suspicions, and the focus turned to rebuilding the facility’s production capacity.
 With no manual work to be done at the now-dormant shipyard, the prisoners were divided up and transferred to work at other camps. (In my case, I ended up at the Ohashi prison camp in the mountains of northern Japan, whose liberation in 1945 I described in  a previous  Quillette article). With a rueful chuckle or two, we left the crippled shipyard and its corps of puzzled Japanese commandants.
 After his release from captivity, Lieutenant-Commander Edward V. Dockweiler of the United States Navy, the senior Allied officer at Camp 3D at the time of our departure, wrote a report on the Nippon Kokan fire for American and Canadian military authorities. It read,  in part:
 About 20:00 hours on 18 January 1944, a large fire broke out in this yard, completely destroying the steel shed, ships outfitting stores, prisoner of war mess hall, riggers lobby, tool rooms, part of the shipfitters shop and mould loft. At this time, the yard was engaged in building escort destroyers and merchant shipping. Its tonnage production was about 8,000 tons per month. The fire was started by Staff Sergeant Clark, Canadian Postal Corps. and Private K.S. Cameron, Royal Canadian Ordnance Corps… This act of sabotage greatly crippled the production of this yard and directly minimized the Japanese war effort, and the contribution to the Allied War effort that these two men made under the handicap of being prisoners of war, cannot be overestimated. Their conduct as prisoners of war, while under my jurisdiction, was exemplary and fulfilled the highest traditions of the Canadian Army.
 Staff Sergeant Clark and Private Cameron escaped detection until the end of their captivity, and both survived to return home to their Canadian families. For their brave actions, they were decorated by the King. “Charlie” Clark won the Distinguished Conduct Medal (DCM) and Private Cameron the Military Medal (MM).
 Clark also became an energetic leader of the community of returning Canadians soldiers, and eventually formed the Hong Kong Veterans Association of Canada. Tragically, soon after returning home, this brave man died in a house fire. And with him perished his detailed notes of how he and Cameron had so bravely carried the war to their enemy.
 Back home in Alberta, Private Cameron became active within his community as a Big Brother, Kiwanis Club member, and Canadian National Institute for the Blind (CNIB) volunteer, in which capacity he taught blind people to play golf and curl. While writing this account, I asked his family about his wartime exploits. They told me that they’d only learned of them when he was decorated and written up in the press. When asked why he hadn’t told them about it previously, Cameron had simply smiled and said he “was just doing my duty” (an admirably humble formulation that, as his 1944-era Japanese captors might have noted, wasn’t literally true).
 Today is Remembrance Day in the British Commonwealth, a time to pay tribute to veterans and their sacrifices. Like others who served in the military, I witnessed many brave and selfless acts. But few were as admirable—and successful—as those of these two captured Canadian soldiers.
   George MacDonell was born in Edmonton, Alberta in 1922. He served in the Royal Rifles of Canada, which deployed to Hong Kong in 1941 as part of C-Force, shortly before Hong Kong’s capture by the Japanese army. More information about his story may be found   here  and   here .
 Featured image: The battleship Yamato, photographed in September 1941, before Japan’s entry into World War II. The Yamato was built at the Kure Naval Arsenal near Hiroshima, not at the Yokohama-Mitsubishi Shipyard facility discussed in this article (of which few historical photographs have survived).
title: 在二战中庆祝两名加拿大战俘拖走了整个日本造船厂
title_en: Celebrating Two Canadian POWs Who Took Down an Entire Japanese Shipyard in WW2

在美国，两党之间越来越多的共识是要控制主导技术公司积累的巨大力量。从州首府到国会，官员们发起了多项调查，调查亚马逊，苹果，Facebook和谷歌四大巨头是否现在是出于弊大于利，其规模和规模是否要求政府采取行动以制止或破坏它们。
 美国监管机构还没有出示所有证件，但他们应该停下来，辩称规模太大等于反竞争，或者试图分拆或大幅重组科技巨头。相反，他们可能希望着眼于欧洲。
 美国和欧盟对大技术的态度长期以来存在分歧。美国监管者和立法者更加关注这些公司的规模，而欧盟则关注与数据控制有关的问题。最近，欧盟起诉亚马逊滥用其客户和供应商数据的优势，以在通过该平台销售的数千家独立企业中获得竞争优势。早些时候，欧盟取消了苹果可疑的税收惯例以及谷歌对其广告平台的管理。它还尝试通过诸如《通用数据保护条例》之类的规则，使个人对他们的数据有更多的控制权，该规则允许个人选择退出Cookie以及访问他们访问的网站上的数据跟踪。
  相比之下，在美国，参议员伊丽莎白·沃伦（Elizabeth Warren）到公开市场协会（Open Markets Institute）等倡导者的强烈声音呼吁反托拉斯执法机构将这些公司解散。周二，参议员理查德·布卢门撒尔（D–Connecticut）敦促“科技巨头分手。因为他们滥用了自己的力量和力量。”在行政部门，司法部反托拉斯事务负责人马坎·德莱希姆（Makan Delrahim）也谈到要拆除大公司。
 对美国政府而言，反托拉斯执法的最大魅力是吸引人，因为美国政府曾在20世纪打破了标准石油公司和AT＆T的记忆，也许是欧洲人才真正提出了真正的问题：公司的使用和滥用建立帝国的数据。正如欧洲委员会执行副总裁玛格丽特·韦斯特格（Margrethe Vestager）在宣布针对亚马逊的行动时所写的那样，“我们对亚马逊的成功及其规模并不感到怀疑。我们关注的是非常具体的商业行为”，即亚马逊使用其数据使自己产品的特权高于其他卖方。
  通过强调数据而不是市场规模为亚马逊提供了不公平的优势，欧盟当局正在应对Big Tech的核心挑战：不是他们的市场价值或他们的积极收购破坏了竞争，而是获得大量数据的途径。通过强行撤资来减少规模或削减其获取能力将满足嗜好，并可能在一定程度上恢复竞争，但除非重组数据市场，否则这一切都是徒劳的。
  在美国，美国司法部最近针对Google提起的反托拉斯诉讼解决了Google特权使用其收集的数据以获取竞争优势的问题。但是，诉讼的语言明确地穿上了1890年的《谢尔曼法》的精神，呼应了过时的规模和市场集中度的口头禅，以某种方式暗示着人们仍对实际问题仍然抱有一定的把握。
随着时间的流逝，美国法律已经从单一角度来看待反托拉斯：对消费者的伤害。对于大技术公司的批评者来说，这是一个问题，因为这些公司免费提供许多产品，并且可以辩称在其他情况下它们会降低价格。美国的反托拉斯框架根本不适合这些21世纪的庞然大物的独特结构和范围。
 用众议院反托拉斯小组委员会工作人员的律师丽娜·汗（Lina Khan）的话说，“反托拉斯的当前框架，特别是将其与消费者福利挂钩的竞争，定义为短期”。长期价格效应，不足以捕捉现代经济中的市场支配力架构。”该报告称，高科技四巨头已经从“草率，劣势的初创公司”发展成为“我们在石油大亨和铁路大亨时代最后一次见到的那种垄断”，并且获得了太多的权力，后来被他们利用。可汗赞成修改法律，以更广泛地看待垄断的不良影响。
  美国一些反托拉斯倡导者所偏爱的直率做法与欧盟的外科手术策略形成鲜明对比。分手在口头上很有吸引力。他们消除了媒体的喧嚣，说：“我们正在做某事！”因此，他们很可能会继续吸引两党。此外，欧盟对谁拥有什么数据以及如何使用数据的关注并没有阻碍大技术公司的崛起。
 但是，如果问题不是本质上的问题，而是特权访问和使用数据，那么欧盟的做法将更适合这些公司，而不是它们类似的20世纪前身。它们不仅仅是标准机油或AT＆T的数字化变体。它们是具有基于数据的独特业务模型的新实体。除非对其数据的使用进行有意义的更改，否则它们是否被破坏甚至内部是否安装了防火墙都没有关系。他们将在数据市场上保持强大的影响力，并对没有相同数据访问权限的小型公司施加反竞争压力。
 欧盟的行动以及为个人提供更多数据控制权的早期努力，认识到新世界比美国基于针对早期工业垄断而制定的反托拉斯法规的行动要好。与其他许多方面一样，美国可以从其他国家的方法中有意义地学习，而不必依赖于自己的历史和框架。
 人们常说数据是新油。作为一种资源，可能是这样。但是就遏制过剩而言，数据和技术公司更像是核能：强大，功能强大，但需要复杂而独特的工具才能善用它们并加以遏制。

There’s a growing bipartisan consensus in the US to rein in the massive power accumulated by dominant tech firms. From state capitals to Congress, officials have launched multiple investigations of whether the big four of  Amazon,  Apple,  Facebook, and  Google are now forces more for harm than good and whether their size and scale demand government action to curtail them or potentially break them up.
 US regulators have not yet shown all their cards, but they should pause before arguing that too big equals anticompetitive, or seeking to break up or substantially restructure the tech giants. Instead, they might want to look to Europe.
 The US and EU have long differed in their approaches to Big Tech. US regulators and legislators have focused more on the size of these companies, while the EU has focused on issues related to control of data. Most recently, the EU  sued Amazon for taking undue advantage of its customer and vendor data to gain a competitive edge over the thousands of independent businesses who sell through the platform. Earlier, the EU chipped away at Apple’s questionable tax practices and Google’s management of its ad platform. It has also attempted to give individuals more control over their data through rules such as the  General Data Protection Regulation, which allows individuals to opt out of cookies and data-tracking on websites they visit.
  In the US, by contrast, powerful voices, from  Senator Elizabeth Warren to advocates such as the Open Markets Institute, call for  antitrust enforcement to  break up these companies. Tuesday, Senator Richard Blumenthal (D–Connecticut)  urged “a break-up of tech giants. Because they've misused their bigness and power.” In the executive branch, Justice Department antitrust chief Makan Delrahim, also has spoken of  dismantling the big companies.
 As appealing as the big stick of antitrust enforcement is to a US government with memories of breaking up Standard Oil and AT&T in the 20th century, it may be the Europeans who are getting to the real issue: the companies’ use, and abuse, of data to erect empires. As European Commission executive vice president Margrethe Vestager wrote in announcing the action against Amazon, “We do not take issue with the success of Amazon or its size. Our concern is a very specific business conduct”—Amazon’s use of its data to privilege its own products over those of other sellers.
  By emphasizing that data, rather than market size, gives Amazon an unfair advantage, the EU authorities are addressing the core challenge of Big Tech: It’s not their market value or their aggressive acquisitions that undermine competition, it’s the access to mountains of data. Reducing their scale through forced divestitures or curtailing their ability to acquire will satisfy bloodlust and may marginally restore competition, but unless the data market is restructured, it may all be for naught.
  In the US, the Justice Department’s recent antitrust  suit against Google addresses Google’s privileged use of data it collects to gain a competitive advantage. But the language of the suit, explicitly donning the mantle of the 1890 Sherman Act, echoes the outdated mantras of size and market concentration in a way that suggests a still-encumbered grasp of the real issue.
  Over time, US law has come to view antitrust through a single lens: harm to the consumer. That’s a problem for critics of Big Tech, because the companies give away many of their products for free and can argue that in other cases they lower prices. The US antitrust framework simply isn’t well-suited to the unique structure and scope of these 21st-century behemoths.
 In the  words of Lina Khan, an attorney who served on the staff of the House antitrust subcommittee that issued a  highly critical report of the tech giants in October, “the current framework in antitrust—specifically its pegging competition to consumer welfare, defined as short-term price effects—is unequipped to capture the architecture of market power in the modern economy.”  The report says tech’s Big Four have gone from being “scrappy, underdog startups” to the “kinds of monopolies we last saw in the era of oil barons and railroad tycoons” and that have acquired too much power that they have then exploited. Khan favors changing the law to look more broadly at the ill effects of monopolies.
  The blunt approach favored by some antitrust advocates in the US contrasts with the surgical tactics of the EU. Breakups are rhetorically appealing; they cut through the media noise and say, “We are doing something!” For that reason, they’re likely to gather continued bipartisan steam. Moreover, the EU’s focus on who owns what data and how it can be used has done little to slow Big Tech’s ascent.
 But if the issue isn’t size per se but rather privileged access to and use of data, the EU approach is more tailored to these companies, rather than the 20th-century antecedents they resemble. They are not simply digital variants of Standard Oil or AT&T. They are new entities with distinct business models built on data. Unless their use of data is altered meaningfully, it won’t matter if they are broken up or even if firewalls are erected inside them. They will retain powerful sway over data marketplaces and exert anti-competitive pressure on smaller companies without the same access to data.
 The EU’s actions, along with the earlier efforts to give individuals more control over their data, recognize that new world better than US actions based on antitrust regulations designed for industrial monopolies of an earlier time. Here as in so much else, the US could meaningfully learn from approaches in other countries rather than falling back on its own history and frameworks.
 It’s often said that data is the new oil; as a resource, that may be. But as for containing excesses, data and tech companies are more like nuclear energy: potent, immensely powerful, but requiring complicated and unique tools to harness them for good and contain them.

如果像伽利略曾经宣称的那样，用自然语言写自然书，那么Covid-19大流行就把这个真相带给了全世界的数学家，他们受到了冠状病毒的迅速传播的刺激。
  今年到目前为止，他们参与了所有事情，包括揭示新型冠状病毒的传染性，彼此之间的距离，感染者可能会传播该病毒多长时间，单一菌株如何从欧洲传播到纽约和然后爆发遍及美国，以及如何“弄平曲线”以挽救数十万人的生命。建模还有助于说服疾病控制和预防中心，该病毒可以通过在空中停留数小时的气溶胶传播和传播。
  目前，许多人都在努力解决一个特别紧迫且棘手的研究领域：对疫苗的最佳推广进行建模。由于起初疫苗供应将受到限制，因此有关由谁获得这些第一剂的决定可以挽救成千上万人的生命。现在至关重要的是，大约有两种候选疫苗有望获得早期结果，其中一种来自辉瑞和BioNTech，另一种来自Moderna，这些疫苗可能非常有效，并且公司可以为此申请食品和药物管理局的紧急授权。
  但是，弄清楚如何分配疫苗（在针对人类的临床试验中有将近50种疫苗）在正确的时间分配给正确的人群是“一个非常复杂的问题，”医学与卫生运营研究中心主任Eva Lee说佐治亚理工学院的护理。 Lee为Zika，埃博拉和流感疫苗和医疗用品的分配策略建模，目前正在研究Covid-19。她说，冠状病毒“比流感更具有感染力，而且致命得多”。 “我们从未受到病毒的挑战。”
  耶鲁大学公共卫生教授霍华德·福尔曼（Howard Forman）说：“上一次我们使用全新的疫苗进行大规模疫苗接种”是天花和脊髓灰质炎。 “我们正在步入一个我们不习惯的领域。”他说，过去几十年中所有其他疫苗都已经测试了多年，或者被引入的速度非常缓慢。
  由于Covid-19对于65岁以上的人以及患有其他健康问题（例如肥胖症，糖尿病或哮喘）的人特别致命，但是由于健康的年轻人更容易康复而迅速广泛传播，因此数学家面临着两个矛盾疫苗建模时的优先事项：应防止死亡或传播缓慢？
  大多数建模者之间的共识是，如果主要目标是降低死亡率，那么官员们必须优先为年龄较大的人接种疫苗，如果他们想减慢传播速度，则必须针对年轻人。
哈佛大学流行病学家马克·利普西奇（Marc Lipsitch）说：“几乎无论如何，您都会得到相同的答案。”他说，先给老人接种疫苗以预防死亡，然后再转移到其他更健康的人群或普通人群。最近的一项研究模拟了Covid-19如何在美国，印度，西班牙，津巴布韦，巴西和比利时的六个国家中传播，并得出结论，如果主要目标是降低死亡率，则应优先考虑60岁以上的成年人直接疫苗接种。 Lipsitch的科罗拉多大学博尔德分校的Daniel Larremore和Kate Bubar及其同事进行的这项研究已作为预印本发表，这意味着该研究尚未得到同行评审。当然，当考虑到Covid-19对少数群体（尤其是黑人和拉丁裔社区）的巨大影响时，优先级的其他考虑也开始起作用。
  西雅图弗雷德·哈钦森癌症研究中心的应用数学家劳拉·马特拉特（Laura Matrajt）在一封电子邮件中表示，大多数建模者都同意“冠状病毒会以光速改变一切”。这包括我们对病毒如何传播，如何攻击身体，同时患有另一种疾病可能增加风险以及导致超级传播者事件的理解。
  到目前为止，该研究已产生了一些令人惊讶的结果。例如，虽然通常优先考虑使用儿童流感疫苗，但专家表示，在美国，对于Covid-19疫苗来说，年幼的儿童应优先考虑，因为到目前为止，年轻人一直是传播的主要动力。 （这不一定在全球范围内都是正确的；例如，在印度，几代人经常在一起生活在较小的空间中，新的研究表明，儿童和年轻人在正在研究的两个州中都传播了许多病毒。）
  此外，一些模型表明，即使仅部分有效的较低疫苗部署，也可以在大流行方面取得重大进展。还有其他一些强调地方感染和传播率的重要性。 Lee表示，他对大流行的起源，毒力和可能的全球轨迹的早期评估非常准确，纽约市如果约有40％的人口接种了疫苗，则可能含有该病毒，因为该病毒在当地的传播率很低（截至11月16日，阳性率略低于3％），并且已经感染了约20％。
  拉雷莫尔说：“拥有抗体的人群比例越高，您付出的代价就越大。”因为您可以优先为没有抗体的人提供疫苗。
  Lee说，所有这些发现都很重要，因为“到头来，您将永远无法为整个人口提供足够的疫苗”，而且并不是所有的美国人都可以使用。事实上，世界卫生组织最近预测，健康的年轻人在老年人，医护人员和其他高危人群接种疫苗后，成年人甚至可能要到2022年才能获得疫苗。
  为了对疫苗的推出进行建模，数学家必须使用住房和社会经济状况，日常习惯，年龄和健康风险等数据，构建反映人类生命爆炸和复杂相互作用的公式。但是首先，他们确定了病毒的传染性-繁殖率或“ R-naught”。这表示可以将一个感染者传染给的人数。
当一部分人（取决于无R的人）具有免疫力时（通过从自然感染中恢复（如果可以免疫）或通过疫苗接种），便实现了畜群免疫。这意味着尽管仍可能发生小规模的爆发，但这种大流行不会再次在全球范围内爆发。鉴于导致Covid-19的SARS-CoV-2病毒很零，世界卫生组织估计，要实现这一目标，必须有65％至70％的人口需要免疫。
  为疫苗铺开建模需要复杂的杂技，而使去年春天迷住公众的曲线平整的模型需要花数周的时间才能完成，而疫苗分发模型则需要数月的时间。建模人员面临着无数的实际挑战。一方面，目前正在准备中的许多疫苗（包括辉瑞，BioNTech和Moderna的两种候选疫苗）需要两次注射，相隔数周，其中涉及注册表和后续操作，以确保人们获得第二次关键的加强注射。正如《纽约时报》在9月下旬指出的那样：“公司可能不得不在运输数千英里的微小玻璃小瓶的同时，在冬天的深处将其保持与南极极低的温度。”
  还有疫苗效力的问题。给定的疫苗在所有人群中都能提供强大的免疫力吗？还是会主要缩短感染时间并减轻症状，这对于降低死亡率和传播仍然具有重要价值？而且，如果在老年人中疫苗效果不佳怎么办？目前，使用信使RNA的疫苗（包括Moderna和Pfizer和BioNTech生产的信使）“在老年人中看起来相当不错”，马里兰大学学校疫苗开发和全球卫生中心主任凯瑟琳·诺伊兹尔（Kathleen Neuzil）表示。医学。对这两种候选疫苗的初步分析表明，它们的有效性可能超过90％。
  最后，还有一个令人困扰的问题，就是感染后免疫能持续多长时间。对于某些病毒，例如引起水痘的水痘带状疱疹病毒，免疫力可持续数十年。对于其他病毒，例如包含SARS-CoV-2和普通感冒的冠状病毒家族，该病毒具有相对较高的突变率，可以保护新菌株免受我们的抗体侵害。这种不确定性很难精确建模，因此许多建模人员认为，至少在目前，被感染者是免疫的。
  西雅图弗雷德·哈钦森癌症中心的马特拉特（Mattrajt）生动地回忆起今年四月她开始与同事合作进行疫苗接种模型时，凭空建立一个模型有多么困难。她回忆说，“不确定因素太多了”。研究人员共同开发了基于惊人的440个左右参数组合的算法，这些参数包括从传播到免疫，到年龄组和死亡率。他们的计算机花了将近9000个小时来运行方程式，他们的模型于8月份作为预印本发布，表明如果一开始的疫苗供应量很少，则要降低死亡率，应该优先考虑老年人。
  但是，对于至少有60％有效性的疫苗，一旦足以覆盖至少一半的人口，则改以年龄在20至50岁的健康个体以及儿童为目标，将死亡人数降至最低。该模型还预测了不同数量的疫苗覆盖率可以避免多少例死亡。例如，如果疫苗中至少有50％有效，那么如果已经有20％的人口已经被感染并且具有免疫力，那么仅对其余35％的疫苗接种就可以将死亡人数减半。
  在Matrajt和她的同事们的模型中，一旦60％的人口免疫了，就能实现畜群免疫。她说：“不同的模型给出不同的数字是完全正常的，”她解释说为什么她的估计与WHO的65％的数字略有不同。
加州大学戴维斯分校的环境和资源经济学家迈克尔·斯普林伯恩（Michael Springborn）说，该模型“在处理大量可能的案例方面做得很好”，他刚刚与加州大学戴维斯分校的同事杰克·巴克纳（Jack Buckner）完成了自己的模型和佐治亚州立大学数学流行病学家Gerardo Chowell。他们在预印本上发表的研究还表明，谨慎地初步确定目标可以减少死亡。
  斯普林博恩说，这些模型表明，即使只对部分人口提供部分有效的疫苗，“也可以大大减少感染和减少死亡。”
  Lee的模型是由她在2003年首次与CDC一起开发的软件创建的，用于分配自然灾害和大流行病中的物资，分析了在感染率不同且疫苗供应最初稀缺的地区可能如何控制该疾病。她的模型预测，在春季遭受重创的纽约市，大约有60％的人口可能需要免疫力才能遏制大流行。假设已经有20％的人被感染，则大约需要接种40％的疫苗。然而，在圣地亚哥的感染率较低的地方，李的模型表明，有65％的人需要通过感染或疫苗接种来获得免疫力。在休斯敦，这一数字可能高达73％，这是因为感染以“慢烧”持续存在，并且是因为该市庞大，脆弱的拉美裔和非裔美国人人口承受了不成比例的风险。
  Lee警告说，这些结果并不意味着您可以突然在休斯敦或纽约百老汇的一场足球比赛中露面，而是意味着，通过持续的预防措施，病毒很可能包含在其模型中给出的百分比中，直到更多。疫苗到了。
  尽管他们的结果各不相同，但大多数模型都认为某些因素至关重要，尤其是年龄组，这改变了感染病毒，感染病毒，传播和死亡的风险。这并非总是可以预见的：例如，猪流感在一定程度上使老年人幸免，而SARS-CoV-2严重影响了65岁以上的老年人。65岁以上的成年人占美国人口的16％，但约占美国人口的80％ 2019冠状病毒病死亡。
  另外，年龄间接影响传播方式。 2009年，耶鲁大学的流行病学家Alison Galvani和Jan Medlock在《科学》杂志上发表了数学模型，结果表明，针对儿童和年轻人（除了老人）使用流感疫苗，可以将猪流感的感染人数从5900万减少到4400万；对于季节性流感，8,300万感染病例可能降至4,400万。事实证明，儿童导致大量的流感传播，保护儿童可以​​保护整个社会。
  这项研究以及其他类似的研究激发了疾病预防控制中心政策的变化，以优先为儿童接种疫苗。 “这是我们对疫苗的思考方式的一次革命，” Larremore说。现在，疫苗接种模型通常会考虑通过对最易感染人群进行疫苗接种来间接保护最弱势人群。
年龄也以复杂的方式与不同地区的社会联系相交。例如，在美国，非裔美国人和拉丁美洲人社区受到Covid-19袭击的比例不成比例，部分原因是多代人在一起生活的普遍程度：年长的人更容易接触到年轻人，而年轻人可能是最有可能携带艾滋病的人。感染。
  对连通性进行建模需要绘制网格，以表示我们彼此之间的生活和运动方式。 2008年，一篇具有里程碑意义的论文建立了一个网格，如今，各地的流行病学家仍在使用该网格。根据年龄，从出生到70岁及以上，人们可以分为几类。在这项研究中，有超过7,000个人在一天的过程中保留了自己的联系人日记（其中将近98,000）。联系人按地点（家庭，学校，工作，休闲）和自然（身体或非身体，短暂或更长时间）分类。该模型发现，当一种新病原体开始在完全易感人群中传播时，5至19岁的人群往往会出现最高的感染率，这可能是因为他们与他人的接触更加频繁和物理。它还显示了社会的联系网格如何深刻影响传播。
  该模型于2017年在全球范围内扩展，与152个国家/地区的联系率。 Matrajt说：“这就是我们所有人所用的，因为这是我们最好的方式来识别人们之间的联系方式。”她将联系人网格合并到模型中。
  例如，“如果孩子真的是建立社会的枢纽，”拉雷莫尔说，“因此，如果给孩子接种疫苗，您会分散传播网络，那么这将为我们提供一种完全不同的方式来推广这种疫苗。”
  原始网格依赖于日记。今天，我们通过实时手机和在线活动收集数据的能力可能会更大。
  斯普林伯恩说，当去年春天社会隔离现象普遍出现时，它极大地改变了典型的传播模型的输入。华盛顿大学健康指标与评估研究所的数据表明，社会隔离在减少传播方面具有巨大作用。 Springborn在一封电子邮件中写道，以前的研究中的联系方式是“来自大流行前时期”。 “我们知道，在社会距离遥远的情况下，联系率有很大不同，我们想对此加以考虑。而且，我们预计随着感染数量的下降，社会隔离也会逐渐减弱。人性：随着风险的降低，减轻风险的行为也随之降低。”
  这也需要建模。它将影响对疫苗推出和成功的期望。实际上，Lee坚持认为，如果我们现在90％遵守口罩和社交隔离，我们可以不用疫苗就可以感染病毒。
在Springborn，Buckner和Chowell进行的研究中，通过为基本工人和非必需工人创建按年龄分层的类别来模拟社会隔离。基本工人（卫生保健工作者，食品杂货工人和许多学校老师等）由于没有社交距离，因此感染风险很高。该模型发现，当优先考虑优先接种疫苗的人员时，死亡以及总寿命损失将大大减少。作者认为，如果目标是最大程度地减少死亡，则应优先考虑40至59岁之间的老年基本工人。
  斯普林博恩说，如果没有疫苗，到2021年的头六个月中大约有179,000人可能死亡。他的小组的模型表明，仅通过逐步引入疫苗，每月将其提供给10％的人口，并均匀分配而不优先考虑任何组，就可以将死亡人数降低至约8.8万。但是，根据人们的年龄以及他们是否是必需的工作者，有针对性地分发疫苗可以根据情况而挽救7,000至37,000条生命。
  除日记和手机数据外，还有其他方法可以改善社交联系。人口普查和其他数据反映了年龄，职业和社会经济地位，Lee在模型中包括了这些数据。她说：“邮政编码为您提供了大量信息。”有关疾病患病率和住院情况的公共卫生数据可以弄清楚Covid-19患者所患有的其他无关疾病以及给定地区的脆弱性。甚至有关城市住房的信息，无论是摩天大楼还是单户住宅，都可以提供线索，说明人们聚集在一起的紧密程度以及互动的可能性。输入此类数据可实现对当地条件敏感的疫苗推出。李说，她需要为美国大约500个代表性城市建模，以准确覆盖整个国家。
  尽管模型功能强大，但它们是不完善的指南。它们不可避免地与深刻而广泛的社会问题相交。大流行严重伤害和杀死了少数族裔和低收入者。因此，芬兰卫生与福利研究所传染病控制和预防接种科副主任，世界卫生组织SAGE工作成员Hanna Nohynek认为，各个小组正在研究应制定疫苗分配框架的伦理原则。 Covid-19疫苗小组。
  在美国，美国国家科学，工程和医学研究院已经开始为疫苗的公平分配建立模型。此外，还出现了另外两种重要的模式，一种与宾夕法尼亚大学医学院有关，另一种与约翰·霍普金斯大学有关。两者都受到对道德，公平，最大化利益，建立信任和更大公共利益的关注。
  但是，建立信任在实践中可能会充满挑战。例如，众所周知，与白人相比，黑人的住院和死亡率高得多。然而，当伦理学家开始谈论优先考虑黑人使用疫苗时，可以认为将他们推向高潮是对他们进行试验的意图。如果有非裔美国人的担忧，那是对“医疗领域数百年来滥用非洲裔美国人的悠久历史”做出的合理反应，医学伦理学家哈里埃特·华盛顿（Harriet Washington）说。
  最终，道德和数学模型都必须面对现实世界的现实。 “这很难，因为数学本质上是沸腾的
note_en:
If the book of nature is written in the language of mathematics, as Galileo once declared, the Covid-19 pandemic has brought that truth home for the world's mathematicians, who have been galvanized by the rapid spread of the coronavirus.
  So far this year, they have been involved in everything from revealing  how contagious the novel coronavirus is, how  far we should stand from each other, how long an infected person  might shed the virus, how a single strain  spread from Europe to New York and then  burst across America, and how to ‘' flatten the curve’' to save hundreds of thousands of lives. Modeling also helped persuade the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention that the virus  can be airborne and transmitted by  aerosols that stay aloft for hours.
  And at the moment many are grappling with a particularly urgent—and thorny—area of research: modeling the optimal rollout of a vaccine. Because vaccine supply will be limited at first, the decisions about who gets those first doses could save tens of thousands of lives. This is critical now that promising early results are coming in about two vaccine candidates—one  from Pfizer and BioNTech and one  from Moderna—that may be highly effective and for which the companies may apply for emergency authorization from the Food and Drug Administration.
  But figuring out how to allocate vaccines—there are close to 50 in clinical trials on humans—to the right groups at the right time is “a very complex problem,” says  Eva Lee, director of the Center for Operations Research in Medicine and Health Care at the  Georgia Institute of Technology. Lee has  modeled dispensing strategies for vaccines and medical supplies for Zika, Ebola, and  influenza, and is now working on Covid-19. The coronavirus is “so infectious and so much more deadly than influenza,” she says. “We have never been challenged like that by a virus.”
  Howard Forman, a public health professor at Yale University, says “the last time we did mass vaccination with completely new vaccines,'' was with smallpox and polio. "We are treading into an area we are not used to.” All the other vaccines of the last decades have either been tested for years or were introduced very slowly, he says.
  Because Covid-19 is especially lethal for those over 65 and those with other health problems such as obesity, diabetes, or asthma, and yet is spread rapidly and widely by healthy young adults who are more likely to recover, mathematicians are faced with two conflicting priorities when modeling for vaccines: Should they prevent deaths or slow transmission?
  The consensus among most modelers is that if the main goal is to slash mortality rates, officials must prioritize vaccinating those who are older, and if they want to slow transmission, they must target younger adults.
  “Almost no matter what, you get the same answer,” says Harvard epidemiologist  Marc Lipsitch. Vaccinate the elderly first to prevent deaths, he says, and then move on to other, healthier groups or the general population. One recent study modeled how Covid-19 is likely to spread in six countries—the U.S., India, Spain, Zimbabwe, Brazil, and Belgium—and concluded that if the primary goal is to reduce mortality rates, adults over 60 should be prioritized for direct vaccination. The study, by  Daniel Larremore and  Kate Bubar of the University of Colorado Boulder, Lipsitch, and their colleagues, has been published as a  preprint, meaning it has not yet been peer reviewed. Of course, when considering Covid-19's outsized impact on minorities—especially Black and Latino communities—additional considerations for prioritization come into play.
  Most modelers agree that “everything is changing with coronavirus at the speed of light,” as applied mathematician  Laura Matrajt, a research associate at the Fred Hutchinson Cancer Research Center in Seattle, put it in an email. That includes our understanding of how the virus spreads, how it attacks the body, how having another disease at the same time might raise the risk, and what leads to super-spreader events.
  So far, the research has yielded some surprising results. While children are usually prioritized for flu vaccine, for example, experts say the very young should be a lower priority for Covid-19 vaccines in the United States, because thus far young adults have been primary drivers of transmission. (This is not necessarily true across the globe; in India, for instance, where multiple generations often live together in smaller spaces, new research shows both  children and young adults are spreading much of the virus in the two states studied.)
  In addition, several models suggest that significant headway can be made against the pandemic even with lower deployment of a vaccine that is only partly effective. And several others emphasize the importance of local infection and transmission rates. According to Lee, whose early assessments of the pandemic's origin, virulence, and probable global trajectory proved to be  strikingly accurate, New York could potentially contain the virus if about 40 percent of the population were vaccinated, because local transmission of the virus is  fairly low (a positivity rate of a little below 3 percent as of Nov. 16), and around 20 percent have already been infected.
  “The higher the fraction of people in the population who already have antibodies, the more bang for your buck,” says Larremore, because you can prioritize giving vaccines to those who don’t have antibodies.
  All these findings are important because, “at the end of the day, you will never have enough vaccines for the entire population," says Lee—and not all Americans will take it. In fact, the World Health Organization recently predicted that healthy young adults may not even be able to get a vaccine until 2022, after the elderly, health care workers, and other high-risk groups are vaccinated.
  To model the rollout of vaccines, mathematicians must build formulas that reflect the starburst of human life and our complex interactions, using data like housing and socioeconomic status, daily habits, age, and health risks. But first they establish how contagious the virus is—its reproductive rate, or "R-naught." This represents the number of people that one infected person can be expected to transmit the infection to.
  When some fraction (depending on R-naught) of people are immune (either by recovering from natural infection, if that grants immunity, or through vaccination), herd immunity has been achieved. That means that while small outbreaks may still occur, the pandemic will not take off globally again. Given the R-naught of SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes Covid-19, the World Health Organization has estimated that 65 percent to 70 percent of the population needs to be immune before this can be achieved.
  Modeling vaccine rollout requires a complex acrobatics, and while the models to flatten the curve that mesmerized the public last spring took weeks to craft, vaccine distribution models take many months. There are innumerable practical challenges facing modelers. For one thing, many of the vaccines currently in the pipeline—including the two candidates from  Pfizer and BioNTech and  Moderna—require two shots, several weeks apart, which involve registries and follow-up to ensure that people get the second, critical booster shot. And as The New York Times  noted in late September, “Companies may have to transport tiny glass vials thousands of miles while keeping them as cold as the South Pole in the depths of winter.”
  There is also the question of vaccine efficacy. Will a given vaccine provide robust immunity, and in all groups? Or will it primarily shorten duration of infection and lessen symptoms, which would still be of great value in reducing mortality as well as transmission? And what if a vaccine is less effective among the elderly, as is often the case? At the moment, vaccines using  messenger RNA (including those produced by Moderna and Pfizer and BioNTech) are “looking pretty good in older adults,” according to  Kathleen Neuzil, director of the Center for Vaccine Development and Global Health at the University of Maryland School of Medicine. Preliminary analyses of both vaccine candidates show that they may be more than 90 percent effective.
  Finally, there is also the vexing question of how long immunity might last after infection. For some viruses, such as the varicella-zoster virus that causes chickenpox, immunity can last for decades. For others, such as the family of coronaviruses that includes SARS-CoV-2 and the common cold, the virus has a relatively high mutation rate that  may protect novel strains from our antibodies. That uncertainty is difficult to model precisely, so many modelers assume that, for the time being at least, those who have been infected are immune.
  Matrajt, of the Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center in Seattle, remembers vividly how hard it was to begin to construct a model out of thin air when she began working with colleagues on a vaccination model this past April. There were “so many uncertainties,” she recalls. Together, the researchers developed algorithms based on an astonishing 440 or so combinations of parameters, from transmission to immunity to age groups and mortality. Their computers spent nearly 9,000 hours running equations, and their model,  published in August as a preprint, shows that if there is only a low supply of vaccine at first, older adults should be prioritized if the goal is to reduce deaths.
  But for vaccines that are at least 60 percent effective, once there is enough to cover at least half the population, switching to target healthy individuals ages 20 to 50 as well as children would minimize deaths. The model also predicts how many deaths can be averted with different amounts of vaccine coverage. For instance, if 20 percent of the population has already been infected and is immune, deaths could be halved by vaccinating just 35 percent of the remainder, if the vaccine is at least 50 percent effective.
  In the model by Matrajt and her colleagues, herd immunity is achieved once 60 percent of the population is immune. “It is completely normal that different models will give different numbers,” she says, explaining why her estimate varies slightly from the WHO figure of 65 percent.
  The model does “a really nice job looking at a large number of plausible cases,” says  Michael Springborn, an environmental and resource economist at the University of California, Davis, who just finished his own model with  Jack Buckner, a colleague at UC Davis, and  Gerardo Chowell, a mathematical epidemiologist at Georgia State University. Their  study, released in preprint, also suggests the power of careful initial targeting in reducing deaths.
  The models suggest that even a partially-effective vaccine given to just part of the population, says Springborn, “can go a really long way to reducing infections and reducing deaths.”
  Lee's modeling, created with  software she first developed in 2003, in conjunction with the CDC, for dispensing of supplies in natural disasters and pandemics, analyzes how the disease might be contained in areas with different infection rates and initially scarce vaccine supplies. In New York City, which was hit so hard in the spring, her model predicts that roughly 60 percent of the population may need immunity to contain the pandemic. Assuming 20 percent are already infected, about 40 percent would need to be vaccinated. In San Diego, however, where infection rates have been lower, Lee’s model suggests that 65 percent will need to achieve immunity through infection or vaccination. In Houston, the figure may be as high as 73 percent because the infection has persisted at a “slow burn” and because of the city’s large, vulnerable Latino and African American populations, who have borne disproportionate risk.
  Lee cautions that these results do not mean you can suddenly go to a football game in Houston or Broadway show in New York, but it does mean that with ongoing precautions, the virus might well be contained with the percentages given in her models, until more vaccine arrives.
  Though their results vary, most models agree that certain factors are critical, notably age group, which changes the risk of contracting, spreading, and dying from a virus. It's not always predictable: The swine flu, for instance,  spared older adults to some degree, while SARS-CoV-2 has severely affected those over 65. Adults 65 and older compose 16 percent of the U.S. population but account for about  80 percent of Covid-19 deaths.
  In addition, age indirectly influences transmission patterns. In 2009, Yale epidemiologists Alison Galvani and Jan Medlock published a mathematical model in  Science, showing that targeting flu vaccines to children and young adults (in addition to the elderly) could have slashed swine flu infections from 59 million to 44 million; and for seasonal influenza, 83 million infections could plunge to 44 million. Children, it turns out, drive a disproportionate amount of flu transmission, and protecting them protects society at large.
  The study, and others like it, inspired a change in CDC policy to prioritize vaccinating children. “It was a revolution in how we think about vaccines,” says Larremore. Vaccination models now routinely consider the power of indirect protection of the most vulnerable by vaccinating those most responsible for spread.
  Age also intersects, in complex ways, with social connectivity in different regions. For instance, African American and Latino communities in the United States have been disproportionately hit by Covid-19, in part because of the prevalence of  multiple generations living together: Older individuals are much more exposed to the young adults who might be the likeliest carriers of infection.
  Modeling connectivity requires drawing grids that represent how we live and move among each other. In 2008,  a landmark paper built a grid that epidemiologists everywhere still use today. It stratified people into groups based on age, from birth to 70 years old and up. In the study, more than 7,000 individuals kept a diary of their contacts—nearly 98,000 of them—over the course of one day. Contacts were sorted by place (home, school, work, leisure) and by nature (physical or nonphysical, brief or longer lasting). The model found that 5- to 19-year-olds tend to experience the highest incidence of infection when a new pathogen begins to spread in a completely susceptible population, possibly because of their more frequent and physical contact with others. It also showed how profoundly a society’s grids of connection influence transmission.
  The model was  expanded globally in 2017, with contact rates for 152 countries. “It’s what we all use,” says Matrajt, “because it’s the best thing we have to identify how people contact each other.” She incorporated the contact grid into her model.
  For example, “if kids are really the hubs around which society is built,” Larremore says, “so that if you vaccinate the kids, you fragment that transmission network, then that’s going to give us a totally different way of rolling out this vaccine.”
  The original grid relied on diaries. Today, our ability to gather data through real time cellphone and online activity may be even greater.
  When social distancing became widespread this past spring, it dramatically altered the input into the typical transmission model, says Springborn. Data from the Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation at the University of Washington  shows the power of social distancing in reducing transmission. The contact grids in previous studies are “from pre-pandemic times,” Springborn wrote in an email. “We know that contact rates are very different under social distancing and we want to account for that. And we expect social distancing to soften as the number of infections falls. Human nature: As risk falls, so does risk-mitigating behavior.”
  That needs to be modeled as well. And it will influence the expectations for a vaccine’s rollout and success. In fact, Lee maintains, if we had 90 percent compliance with face masks and social distancing right now, we could contain the virus without a vaccine.
  In the study by Springborn, Buckner, and Chowell, social distancing is modeled by creating age-stratified categories for both essential and nonessential workers. Essential workers—health care workers, grocery workers, and many schoolteachers, among others—are at high risk for infection because they cannot socially distance. This model finds that deaths, as well as total years of life lost, are dramatically decreased when essential workers are prioritized to receive the vaccine. Older essential workers between 40 and 59 should be prioritized first if the goal is to minimize deaths, the authors maintain.
  With no vaccine, about 179,000 people may die in the first six months of 2021, Springborn says. His team’s model suggests that deaths could decline to about 88,000 simply by introducing a vaccine gradually, giving it to 10 percent of the population each month, and distributing it uniformly without prioritizing any groups. But distributing vaccines in a targeted way, based on people’s ages and whether they are essential workers, could save another 7,000 to 37,000 lives, depending on the situation.
  There are other methods of teasing out social connectivity beyond diaries and cellphone data. Census and other data reflect age, profession, and socioeconomic status, and Lee includes them in her models. “The zip code gives you a huge amount of information,” she says. Public health data on disease prevalence and hospitalizations can tease out the other unrelated diseases that Covid-19 patients have, as well as vulnerabilities in a given area. Even information on a city’s housing, whether skyscrapers or single-family homes, can give a clue to how closely people are packed together and how likely they are to interact. Inputting this kind of data allows for a vaccine rollout that is sensitive to local conditions. Lee would need to model about 500 representative cities around the U.S., she says, to cover the country accurately.
  As powerful as the models can be, they are an imperfect guide. Inevitably they intersect with deep and broad social concerns. The pandemic has disproportionately harmed and killed minorities and those with lower incomes. For that reason, various groups are looking into the ethical principles that should frame vaccine allocation, according to  Hanna Nohynek, deputy head of the Infectious Diseases Control and Vaccinations Unit at the Finnish Institute for Health and Welfare, and a member of the WHO’s SAGE Working Group on Covid-19 vaccines.
  In the U.S., the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine has  begun to model an equitable allocation of a vaccine. In addition, two other important models have emerged,  one associated with University of Pennsylvania School of Medicine, and  the other with Johns Hopkins University. Both are guided by concerns about ethics, fairness, maximizing benefits, building trust and the greater public good.
  But building trust can be challenging in practice. For instance, it’s widely acknowledged that Black people have experienced hospitalization and death at disproportionately high rates compared to White people. Yet when ethicists begin to talk about prioritizing Black people for vaccines, it can be perceived as an intent to experiment on them by pushing them to the head of the line. If there is concern among African Americans, it’s a logical reaction to “a vast history of centuries of abuse of African Americans in the medical sphere,” says medical ethicist Harriet Washington, author of “ Medical Apartheid.”
  Ultimately, both ethical and mathematical models have to face real-world practicalities. “It’s hard because math essentially boils do
